U4L10 - Ensemble Learning

Type date here

Front Matter

#Delete all objects from Environment - Use with caution
remove(list = ls()) 

#Load Libraries
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.1     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(rpart)
library(rattle) #Include before randomForest so that randomForest::importance is not masked by rattle::importance
## Loading required package: bitops
## Rattle: A free graphical interface for data science with R.
## Version 5.6.2 Copyright (c) 2006-2023 Togaware Pty Ltd.
## Type 'rattle()' to shake, rattle, and roll your data.
library(randomForest)
## randomForest 4.7-1.2
## Type rfNews() to see new features/changes/bug fixes.
## 
## Attaching package: 'randomForest'
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:rattle':
## 
##     importance
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     combine
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2':
## 
##     margin
#Read in Data
titanic <- read.csv("../../sample_data/L08_titanic3.csv")

Example - Generating a Bootstrap Sample in R

Part a - Generate a bootstrap sample using the first 10 rows and 4 columns of Titanic with a seed of 123

#Create a temp copy of the dataset using the first 10 rows and first 4 columns
tempTitanic <- titanic[1:10, 1:4]
tempTitanic

#Sample the row numbers with replacement
set.seed(123)
bs1Ind <- sample(1:nrow(tempTitanic), nrow(tempTitanic), replace = TRUE)
set.seed(NULL)

#View the select rows 
table(bs1Ind)

#View the dataset
bs1Train <- tempTitanic[bs1Ind, ]

The in-bag observations are: In bag observations are the ones that are in the sample.

The out-of-bag (OOB) observations are: Out of bag observations are the ones that were in the origional data but not in the bootstrapped sample

Example - Bagging Regression Trees

#Generate First Bootstrap Sample
set.seed(123)
bs1Ind <- sample(1:nrow(titanic), nrow(titanic), replace = TRUE)
set.seed(NULL)

bs1Train <- titanic[bs1Ind, ]

#Train a Regression Tree
regTree1 <- rpart(Age ~ ., data = bs1Train, method = "anova")
  
#Plot Tree
fancyRpartPlot(regTree1, cex = 0.7)

#Generate Second Bootstrap Sample
set.seed(1234)
bs2Ind <- sample(1:nrow(titanic), nrow(titanic), replace = TRUE)
set.seed(NULL)

bs2Train <- titanic[bs2Ind, ]

#Train a Regression Tree
regTree2 <- rpart(Age ~ ., data = bs2Train, method = "anova")

#Plot Tree
fancyRpartPlot(regTree2, cex = 0.7)

#Generate Third Bootstrap Sample
set.seed(23)
bs3Ind <- sample(1:nrow(titanic), nrow(titanic), replace = TRUE)
set.seed(NULL)

bs3Train <- titanic[bs3Ind, ]

#Train a Regression Tree
regTree3 <- rpart(Age ~ ., data = bs3Train, method = "anova")

#Plot Tree
fancyRpartPlot(regTree3, cex = 0.7)

Example - Random Forest in R using Titanic Data

Part a - Prepare the data by performing an 80/20 training/validation split using a seed of 123.

#Perform an 80/20 training/validation split
set.seed(123)
trainInd <- sample(1:nrow(titanic), floor(0.8 * nrow(titanic)))
set.seed(NULL)

Train <- titanic[trainInd, ]
Validation <- titanic[-trainInd, ]

Part b - Build a random forest for predicting whether a person survives based on the other variables in the dataset. The forest should consist of 500 trees. Each time a split is considered, use 3 variables. Explain the error message

rfModel <- randomForest(Survived ~ ., data = Train, ntree = 500, mtry = 3) #mytry is the number of features, the m number of variables to try 
## Warning in randomForest.default(m, y, ...): The response has five or fewer
## unique values.  Are you sure you want to do regression?
## Warning in mean.default(y): argument is not numeric or logical: returning NA
## Error in `y - ymean`:
## ! non-numeric argument to binary operator

Part c - Fix the code to eliminate the error message and train the model.

rfModel <- randomForest(as.factor(Survived) ~ ., data = Train, ntree = 500, mtry = 3)

Part d - What are the default options for ntree and mtry?

The default for mtry depends on whether it is a classification or regression task. The help documentation has a lot of the details.

Part e - Find the probabilities of survival for the first 6 passengers in the validation set. Are you getting the same values?

#Obtain Predicted Probabilities
predProb <- predict(rfModel, newdata = Validation, type = "prob")

#Display Predicted Probabilities for first 6 passengers
head(predProb)
##       No   Yes
## 1  0.998 0.002
## 3  0.636 0.364
## 7  0.748 0.252
## 12 0.330 0.670
## 15 0.210 0.790
## 18 1.000 0.000

  • The probabilities may differ from what is shown on the handout. Also, if you rerun the code, the probabilities may change.

  • In random forest, there is randomness involved each time we build a tree since we have to select a bootstrap sample. If mtry is less than p (number of variables in dataset), there is also a random selection of mtry variables each time we consider making a split.

  • To get reproducible results, use a seed.

Part f - Using a seed of 123 for constructing the forest, report the confusion matrix for the testing data and the overall accuracy of the method.

#Build model using a seed of 123
set.seed(123)
rfModel <- randomForest(as.factor(Survived) ~ ., 
                        data = Train, 
                        ntree = 500, 
                        mtry = 3)
set.seed(NULL)

#Obtain predicted probabilities - not needed for confusion matrix but included to show we all get same probabilities when using the same seed
predProb <- predict(rfModel, newdata = Validation, type = "prob")
head(predProb)
##       No   Yes
## 1  0.996 0.004
## 3  0.612 0.388
## 7  0.770 0.230
## 12 0.314 0.686
## 15 0.222 0.778
## 18 0.998 0.002
#To create the confusion matrix, we need to determine which class (No or Yes) is more likely. Although we could define a threshold and compare probabilities as we have done in the past, things are more challenging if y has more than 2 classes. So, let R pick for us.
predSurv <- predict(rfModel , newdata = Validation, type = "response")
  
#Create confusion matrix
table(predSurv, Validation$Survived)
##         
## predSurv No Yes
##      No  92  23
##      Yes 14  49
#Calculate accuracy
mean(Validation$Survived == predSurv)
## [1] 0.7921348

Part g - How does the confusion matrix from the last problem differ from rfModel$confusion?

#Display Confusion Matrix
rfModel$confusion
##      No Yes class.error
## No  395  44   0.1002278
## Yes  72 198   0.2666667
#Calculate accuracy
tempConfMat <- rfModel$confusion
sum(tempConfMat[1,1] + tempConfMat[2,2])/sum(tempConfMat[1:2,1:2])
## [1] 0.8363893

  • The accuracy values are different.

  • The total number of observations in this confusion matrix is more than the number of observations in the previous confusion matrix.

  • The confusion matrix in this problem is based on predictions using the OOB data from the Training set. The earlier confusion matrix was based on the Validation set.

Example - Random Forest Variable Importance

Part a - Generate the variable importance values.

#Build a random forest classifier using a seed of 123 and generate the variable importance values
set.seed(123)
rfModel <- randomForest(as.factor(Survived) ~ ., 
                        data = Train, 
                        ntree = 500, 
                        mtry = 3,
                        importance = TRUE)
set.seed(NULL)

Part b - Extract the variable importance values

#Extract the importance values from the randomForest model object
rfModel$importance
##                   No        Yes MeanDecreaseAccuracy MeanDecreaseGini
## Pclass   0.036931920 0.12814243          0.071609042         33.57867
## Sex      0.105872033 0.20031397          0.141680920         83.24320
## Age      0.044598457 0.04764105          0.045674947         69.11592
## Siblings 0.020312316 0.00994687          0.016384008         16.63690
## Parch    0.007242857 0.01149273          0.008886679         10.20615
## Fare     0.043739018 0.10153287          0.065615610         80.53885
#Extract the unscaled importance values using the importantce() function
importance(rfModel)
##                 No       Yes MeanDecreaseAccuracy MeanDecreaseGini
## Pclass   18.655060 34.928829             41.30628         33.57867
## Sex      67.497153 87.624109             98.40891         83.24320
## Age      24.157107 23.343567             36.43599         69.11592
## Siblings 18.166660  8.214273             20.93938         16.63690
## Parch     7.184154  9.819590             11.69148         10.20615
## Fare     17.888774 29.376226             37.10056         80.53885
#Extract the scaled importance values using the importance() function
importance(rfModel, scale = FALSE)
##                   No        Yes MeanDecreaseAccuracy MeanDecreaseGini
## Pclass   0.036931920 0.12814243          0.071609042         33.57867
## Sex      0.105872033 0.20031397          0.141680920         83.24320
## Age      0.044598457 0.04764105          0.045674947         69.11592
## Siblings 0.020312316 0.00994687          0.016384008         16.63690
## Parch    0.007242857 0.01149273          0.008886679         10.20615
## Fare     0.043739018 0.10153287          0.065615610         80.53885

Part c - Plot the variable importance values using the varImpPlot() function from the randomForest package.

#Plot the Variable Importance Values
varImpPlot(rfModel, n.var = 6)
